LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Sign in Join now
Lee Harding
1y
  • Report this post

What actually happens after you apply for a job? After you click the apply button it feels like your CV has gone into a black hole. Here’s what actually happens. First your CV and any information you filled in as part of your application gets stored against that job record in the Applicant Tracking System. Despite what some people might tell you, the ATS does not scan your CV and decide whether to reject you or not. If you do get automatically rejected that decision is based on the information you provided in the application form or any questions you answered but not your CV. Next the recruiter will review every CV stored against that job record. Yep, recruiters are reading your CV and deciding whether to progress you to the next stage or not. They’ll usually spend about 6 seconds scanning your CV to decide if you meet the minimum requirements or not. If you don’t, they’ll hit the rejection butting and you’ll get an automated rejection email. If you do meet the requirements they’ll get in touch with you to arrange a call to discuss your experience and the role in more detail. Some recruiters are managing up to 50 live roles at any one time with hundreds of applications for each so it can take some time for them to get back to you after you’ve applied. And that’s what happens after you apply for a job, it’s actually a pretty simple process but a lot of people think that the ATS plays a much bigger role than it actually does. Check out the link in my bio for loads of free resources to help you in your job search. And for more no-nonsense job search advice, hit the follow button… Cheers!

112 17 Comments
Like Comment
Transcript
After you click the apply button, it feels like your CV has gone into a black hole. Here's what actually happens. First, you'll CV and any information you filled in as part of your application gets stored against that job record in the applicant tracking system. Despite what some people might tell you, the ATS does not scan your CV and decide whether to reject you or not. If you do get automatically rejected, that decision is based on the information you provided in the application form or any questions you answered, but not your CV. Next, the recruiter will review every CV stored against that job record. Yep, recruiters are reading your CV and deciding whether to progress you to the next stage or not. They'll usually spend about 6 seconds scanning your CV to decide if you meet the minimum requirements or not. If you don't, they'll hit the rejection button and you'll get an automated rejection e-mail. If you do meet the requirements, they'll get in touch with you to arrange a call to discuss your experience and the role in more detail. Some recruiters are managing up to 50 live roles at any one time, with hundreds of applications for each, so it can take some time for them to get back to you after you've applied. And that's what happens. Do you apply for a job? It's actually a pretty simple process, but a lot of people think that the ATS plays a much bigger role than it actually does. Check out the link in my bio for loads of free resources to help you in your job search. And for more no nonsense job search advice, hit the follow button. Cheers.
Codruta Todorut 1y
  • Report this comment

Why not explain fully how the ATS works? It ranks your CV in percentages and puts them in buckets. If you're at 90% or 95% then a recruiter will look at your CV, most likely. If you're below that, then most likely you'll be bulk selected and sent a rejection e-mail. Recruiters do not look at every CV, they might say they do, to justify their employment, but they don't really bother all that much. And yes, you need to optimize your CV and almost copy paste the job description if you want to make it into the 90%. If you look at it from above, the ATS does reject your CV, or it just puts it into a bucket that nobody will ever look at, which is in extent the same thing.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Shanon Raab 1y
  • Report this comment

This is exactly how we do it hear at DAS. Me and my team read every CV to see if it is a fit or in most cases 75% fit then we will reach out to get clarity. We read every CV no matter how many come in. The ATS doesn't do much except store the CV. Hope this helps

The ATS thing is peddled by CV writers who should know better, but can't think what else to say. If anybody is thinking about getting their CV writing by a "professional", use that as a filter to know who to avoid.

I'm glad to hear someone explain this. What happens after you reject my CV? Is that when it goes into a black hole? I'm not convinced the automated email that says "you will keep my details on file and keep me updated about any new roles", is telling the truth.

This. More of this! So many people seem to think that their application is auto-rejected by an ATS or some sort of AI. It's a total fallacy! And anyone offering CV writing services that will help you "beat the ATS", are selling snake oil.

More Relevant Posts

  • Gainrep

    572 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    Job searching is always a competition, not only of skills, but of how effectively you present them. In most cases, your resume goes through two filters: 1. ATS (Applicant Tracking System) - an automated algorithm that scans for specific keywords and selects the most relevant profiles. 2. Recruiters - who spend only about 6-10 seconds deciding if your resume is worth reading. If your resume doesn’t include the right words, you risk failing both filters. That’s why your resume shouldn’t just be written, it should be planned. Here are some overused phrases that may cost you job opportunities, and how to replace them effectively: 1. Instead of “Participated in…” use: • “Implemented” (when referring to a solution) • “Contributed” (when referring to a result) 2. Replace “Helped with…” with: • “Optimized” (if it’s about improving a process) • “Achieved” (if it’s about reaching a goal or outcome) 3. Swap “Worked on…” for stronger verbs like: • “Developed” • “Created” 4. Change “Involved in…” to: • “Led” (when referring to discussions or projects) • “Negotiated” (when referring to terms or agreements) 5. Replace the cliche “Responsible for…” with: • “Delivered” (when describing a project) • “Managed” (when referring to a team) The main issue with many resumes is the use of vague verbs like “participated” or “helped”. To recruiters these words signal a lack of clear achievements or ownership. For ATS systems they’re simply “empty” phrases that may not even be recognized as relevant. Take the time to refine your resume and make every word count. Good luck landing the position you’re aiming for!

    Like Comment

  • Monica Brito
    8mo
    • Report this post

    🛑 Getting No Job Responses? Here's the Painful Reason Why If you're qualified but your applications are disappearing into a black hole, the problem is likely the Applicant Tracking System (ATS)—the robot gatekeeper. Here's a clear example of how it fails candidates: A resume may list "Microsoft Suite" as a core skill, but if the job description explicitly asks for expertise in "Excel, Word, and PowerPoint," the ATS is not smart enough to connect those dots. Because those specific, explicit keywords aren't present, the system automatically sifts out the resume, and a human recruiter never gets the chance to see the application—even for a perfect candidate. The critical rule: You cannot use the same CV for all jobs and expect success. Every single resume must be meticulously keyword-matched to the posting. 💡 How to Use an ATS Checker Effectively: Find a target job posting: Copy the specific job description you are applying for. Upload your resume: Upload your resume as a Word document or paste its content into the checker's text box. (Be aware that most free versions have daily or monthly limits.) Get your score and feedback: The tool will provide a match score and a list of keywords from the job description that are missing from your resume. Tailor your resume: Add the missing keywords and phrases. Focus on using a standard, easy-to-read format with common fonts and clear section headings. Re-check and refine: Scan your updated resume again to see your improved score and ensure you have addressed the tool's suggestions. Getting past the machine is the first, non-negotiable step to getting your interview. Has this keyword challenge impacted your job search? Share your strategies for beating the ATS!

    39 6 Comments

  • Megan Applegate, CPC
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Ever been told that an ATS will automatically "reject" your resume before a human ever sees it? Let's clear the air on that one! ✨ ⬇️ While it’s a common fear, an Applicant Tracking System (ATS) isn't really a rejection bot. Think of it more as a super-organized digital filing cabinet for recruiters. Its main job is to filter, rank, and sort applications to help hiring managers find the most relevant candidates. So, what does an ATS actually do? It organizes information: The system scans your resume for key details like job titles, skills, and dates, then neatly arranges them into a digital profile. It plays a matching game: It compares the words on your resume to the keywords in the job description. The closer the match, the higher your score! It filters for deal-breakers: This is where the "rejection" myth comes from. The ATS can automatically filter out candidates who don't meet non-negotiable requirements, like work authorization or a mandatory certification. An ATS rarely just "rejects" a resume out of nowhere. However, if your resume isn't optimized, it can become practically invisible. Poor formatting (like tables or graphics) can confuse the system, and missing keywords will give you a low match score, burying your application. Your resume's job is to get seen! Make sure it’s tailored with keywords from the job description and uses a clean, simple format. This ensures your professional story gets told loud and clear. 💪 If you are curious what a fully optimized resume consists of, let's chat! Share this with anyone that is job searching and can benefit from this information:) #ATS #ResumeTips #JobSearch #keywords #ResumeOptimization

    49 22 Comments

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • DHARMESH CHOPDA
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    💡 Ever wondered how an ATS filters out resumes during the screening process from a bulk of applications? It’s not always because a candidate isn’t qualified. Sometimes, it’s because of a silent gatekeeper called the Applicant Tracking System (ATS). An ATS is software that scans, sorts, and filters resumes before a recruiter even sees them. It helps companies manage hundreds or even thousands of applications by tracking who applies, matching keywords, and ranking candidates based on how well their resumes align with the job description. The ATS works based on the company’s requirements. The HR or recruiter enters the specific skills, qualifications, and experience they are looking for into the system. Based on these requirements, the ATS filters resumes. To stand out, your resume must be simple, easy to read, and aligned with the job description. It should not have unnecessary extra content or too little information. The right balance helps the ATS recognize you as a good match. So yes, before your resume reaches a human, it has to impress a machine! 🤖 Here’s how you can make your resume ATS-friendly and actually get noticed: ✅ Use a clean, simple format with no tables, columns, or fancy graphics ✅ Include relevant keywords directly from the job description ✅ Stick with standard headings like Work Experience, Education, Skills ✅ Avoid putting key info in headers or footers ✅ Save your file as a .docx or PDF as per job instructions ✅ Spell out acronyms such as “Bachelor of Science (B.S.)” instead of just “B.S.” Many job seekers don’t realize that over 75% of resumes get rejected by the ATS before a recruiter ever opens them. The system often skips complex formats, images, or missing keywords, and that’s why even strong candidates get filtered out. So the next time you apply for a job, remember your first interviewer might not be human! 🎯 Pro tip: Think of your resume as your marketing tool, not your entire life story. It should speak the same language as the job description. If you’re currently looking for new roles or a full-time opportunity, feel free to reach out to me here on LinkedIn. I will be happy to help you with your job search. 💬 Have you ever wondered why your resume didn’t make it past the first screening? Let’s talk in the comments! 👇 #ATS #ResumeTips #JobSearch #CareerAdvice #Recruitment #HRTech #ApplicantTrackingSystem #CareerGrowth #JobSeekers #ResumeWriting #HiringTips #CareerDevelopment #ATSResume #JobApplications #RecruitmentTips #CareerSuccess #JobHunting #InterviewTips #TalentAcquisition #WorkOpportunities #JobSeekers2025 #CareerOpportunities #ProfessionalGrowth #JobTips #HRInsights #HiringProcess #LinkedInTips #JobStrategy #ResumeOptimization #CareerJourney

  • Siti Rohani
    8mo
    • Report this post

    So You’re Applying for Jobs via Email? Let’s be honest, most people think they know how to do it… until their email gets ignored. If you’re currently job hunting or planning to apply soon, keep reading and save this post for later. This could be the difference between “no reply” and “you’re hired!” 1️⃣ Email Subject Line Make it super clear and professional. ✅ Application for [Job Title] – [Your Full Name] ❌ Don’t write things like “Job Application” or “Resume Attached”. Your subject line is your first impression. Keep it clean, specific, and easy to search. 2️⃣ Greeting Let’s ditch “Dear Sir/Madam” or “To whom it may concern.” It feels robotic. Instead, try finding the recruiter’s name via LinkedIn or the company website. If you really can’t find it: ✅ To the [Department Name] Recruitment Team at [Company Name] It instantly feels more personal and thoughtful. 3️⃣ Email Body This is where you show why you’re a great fit and keep it short but meaningful. 💬 Example: I’m writing to apply for the [Position Name] at [Company Name]. I believe I’m a strong fit for this role because I have [Number] years of experience helping [Industry/Function] achieve [Results or Achievements]. Show confidence, not arrogance. You’re not just telling them what you’ve done but you’re showing how it adds value to their team. 4️⃣ Don’t Forget to Mention Your Resume This part matters more than you think! Always tell them you’ve attached your resume and include a soft call to action (CTA). 💬 Example: I’ve also attached my resume for your review. I’d be happy to discuss my experience further, feel free to reach me at [Your Number]. 5️⃣ End with a Professional Signature Wrap it up neatly..include: ✅ Your full name ✅ Email address ✅ Contact number ✅ LinkedIn profile link (not just your username) Example: https://lnkd.in/g4Vu7MFg Make it easy for recruiters to learn more about you, the fewer clicks, the better. And that’s it! Simple, clear and professional. 👌 Not sure if your resume looks professional enough? Use an ATS Resume Checker here https://lnkd.in/gEz6SjCF to make sure your resume passes recruiter screening systems before you send it out!

  • Daryn Edelman, M.A., C.P.R.W
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    If your resume is a checklist of old duties, you are being auto-rejected by A.I. 🤖 = ☠️ The secret to landing interviews isn't just what you did; it's how you prove the value you created. Recruiters don't just "love" these 8 Green Flags—they are required to make it past the 🤖’s. ➡️ Here are the 3 non-negotiables to beat the system and get noticed: 🔴 Keywords from the Job Posting (The ATS Bypass) The Applicant Tracking System (ATS) isn't reading your resume; it's parsing it. This is where most applications die. 🟦 Metric Insight: Studies show that a vast majority of large organizations use an ATS, and a primary function is to rank candidates based on keyword match percentage. 🔴 The Fix: Don't just list skills; incorporate the exact terminology and phrasing from the job description directly into your experience narratives. This high-score strategy ensures your profile makes it past the #ATS and into human hands. 🔴 Quantifiable Achievements (The ROI Differentiator) "Responsible for social media" is weak. "Grew social media engagement by 21% in 6 months" is a hiring manager's dream. 🟦 Metric Insight: Recruiters look for metrics because numbers prove impact. Using the formula: Action Verb + Task + Result (with numbers) makes your value tangible. 🔴 The Fix: Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) for every key bullet. Focus on revenue generated, costs saved, time reduced, or percentages increased. This is the evidence that converts a screening call into an interview invitation. 🔴 Tailored to the Job Description (The Ultimate Strategy) A generic #resume is a guaranteed fast-track to the 'No' pile. Your resume should look like the perfect match for this specific job. 🟦 The Fix: The job description is your cheat sheet. Your chosen format must be clear and concise (Flag #1), and your most relevant experiences must be highlighted first. Alignment demonstrates genuine interest, professionalism, and shows the recruiter that their search is over. 🏅Master these 8 points, and you transform your resume from a historical document into a compelling business case for hiring you. 🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🟥   🆘  Is your resume not giving you the results you want? Contact me for a free analysis. ♻️  Was this helpful? Repost and pay it forward. Let’s help each other get hired! #InterviewTips #resumetips #jobsearch #ats #hiring

  • Allison Austin
    9mo
    • Report this post

    It's a story we hear too often in our office: "I've applied to so many jobs and haven't even received an interview." If this sounds familiar, it's time to stop the cycle. Ditch the old lists of job duties and start showcasing your achievements. A great resume is your ticket to an interview, but it needs to be more than just a list of tasks. Here's some advice: •Tailor your resume for each job. Highlight the skills and experiences that directly match what the company is looking for. •Optimize for ATS. Many companies use Applicant Tracking Systems. Make sure your resume includes the right keywords to get past the software and into the hands of a hiring manager. •Focus on your accomplishments. Use numbers and specific examples to show the impact you made in previous roles. Ready to land more interviews? Let's get to work on a resume that gets you noticed. #ResumeTips #JobSearch #CareerAdvice #Hiring #GetHired #PrideStaff

    How to Write a Resume That Gets Interviews http://theresumewriters.com.au

  • Hareesh Vemasani
    9mo
    • Report this post

    The modern job application process is broken. And it's rigged against you. You spend hours crafting the perfect resume. You pour your heart into that cover letter. You hit submit with hope. Then... silence. It's not your fault. Most job seekers have no idea they're fighting an invisible enemy: the Applicant Tracking System. Before any human sees your resume, an algorithm decides your fate. These ATS gatekeepers reject 75% of applications before they reach a recruiter's desk. The cruel irony? Your qualifications might be perfect for the role. But if your resume doesn't speak "ATS language," you're eliminated instantly. Here's how the rigged game works: → ATS scans for exact keyword matches from the job description → Formatting that looks great to humans confuses the software → Skills sections get parsed incorrectly → Your experience gets misinterpreted or ignored entirely The solution isn't to give up. It's to learn the rules. Start by reverse-engineering job descriptions. Extract the exact keywords and phrases they use. Mirror their language in your resume. Use standard section headers like "Work Experience" instead of creative ones like "My Journey." Save fancy formatting for the interview. ATS systems prefer clean, simple layouts. Most importantly, tailor every single application. Generic resumes are ATS poison. The hiring process may be rigged, but you can still win. You just need to know how to play the game. What's your biggest frustration with the job application process?
